summary:
Among the earliest copper axes yet discovered are examples from Balkan Chalcolithic sites dating to approximately 5500–5000 BC. These objects, found in present-day Serbia, Bulgaria, and Romania, were produced by casting molten copper into open stone or clay molds — a significant technical advance over the hammering of native copper. The shaft-hole axe form, in which a hole is cast or perforated to receive a wooden handle, appears early in this tradition and became a widely distributed tool and prestige object across Chalcolithic Europe and the Near East.

Copper axes were simultaneously practical tools and markers of social status. Their relative scarcity and the skill required to produce them meant that possession of a copper axe signaled access to exchange networks and specialist craftspeople. Many copper axes from this period have been found in hoards or elite burials rather than domestic contexts, suggesting their function as prestige items was at least as important as their utility. The distribution of early copper axes traces the outlines of exchange networks connecting [[event:3597|Copper Age Balkan]] communities with neighbors across a broad region of temperate Europe.
